title: Today's Tech Brief: Nvidia's Future Chips and the Star Trek Computer
summary: Nvidia is planning N2X and N3X chips with the goal of achieving a Star Trek-like computer. I explore what this means for tech.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Nvidia is already planning N2X and N3X chips — the goal is the Star Trek computer
imageCaption: Nvidia is already planning N2X and N3X chips — the goal is the Star Trek computer (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. I'm looking at some fresh news from Nvidia that's got everyone buzzing. Apparently, they're already planning their next-gen chips, N2X and N3X, with a lofty goal in mind - creating a computer like the one you'd find on Star Trek. Yes, that Star Trek.

According to The Verge, Nvidia's CEO compared their progress to Scotty talking to a mouse - a nod to the iconic Star Trek character who often worked computer magic. What's happening here is Nvidia is pushing the boundaries of what's possible with computing power and AI. Their current RTX technology is just the beginning.

The idea behind these chips is to significantly upgrade computing capabilities, making them more efficient and powerful. This could mean a major shift in how we interact with technology, from smartphones to data centers. Imagine having a computer that anticipates your needs and responds like a trusted assistant.

In other news, there are rumors swirling about Microsoft's plans to integrate AI into their Office software. This could change the game for productivity and collaboration. I'm excited to see how these tools will help us work smarter, not harder.

Nvidia is already planning N2X and N3X chips — the goal is the Star Trek computer, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Tech News Microsoft Nvidia is already planning N2X and N3X chips — the goal is the Star Trek computer “It’s like Scotty talking to that mouse,” says Nvidia’s CEO. “It’s like Scotty talking to that mouse,” says Nvidia’s CEO. by Sean Hollister Jun 3, 2026, 8:03 PM UTC Jensen Huang, chief executive officer of Nvidia Corp. Bloomberg via Getty Images Part Of Com... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
